---
title: "PHREEQC from Scratch #6: Pyrite Oxidation and AMD Formation"
date: 2026-04-12
categories: [Geochemistry, PHREEQC, Groundwater, Environment, English]
series: "PHREEQC from Scratch"
description: "Simulate the formation of Acid Mine Drainage (AMD) caused by the irreversible oxidation of pyrite. Learn how to use the REACTION block and INCREMENTAL_REACTIONS to model stepwise pH drops and goethite precipitation."
image: thumbnail.png
author: "DeepFlow"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
number-sections: false
code-fold: false
lang: en
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <link rel="alternate" hreflang="ja" href="https://deepflows.net/posts/phreeqc/phreeqc-part6/" />
      <link rel="alternate" hreflang="en" href="https://deepflows.net/posts/phreeqc/phreeqc-part6/index-en.html" />
---

## What is Acid Mine Drainage (AMD)?

**Acid Mine Drainage (AMD)** is one of the most severe environmental problems associated with mining. It occurs when sulfide minerals—most notably Pyrite ($\mathrm{FeS_2}$)—are exposed to oxygen and water, oxidizing to produce highly acidic, metal-rich runoff.

```{=html}
<div style="background:#FFF1F2; border:1px solid #FECDD3; border-left:4px solid #E11D48; border-radius:0 8px 8px 0; padding:1.1rem 1.5rem; margin:1.5rem 0;">
  <div style="font-weight:500; color:#9F1239; margin-bottom:0.4rem;">Characteristics of AMD</div>
  <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; gap:0.8rem; font-size:0.85rem;">
    <div style="color:#BE123C;">pH: 1 to 3 (Strongly Acidic)</div>
    <div style="color:#BE123C;">High Fe and SO₄ Concentrations</div>
    <div style="color:#BE123C;">Reddish-brown Precipitates (Goethite)</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

## The Chemistry of Pyrite Oxidation

The oxidation of pyrite proceeds in sequential stages:

**Stage 1: Direct Oxidation by** $\mathrm{O_2}$

$$\mathrm{FeS_2 + \frac{7}{2}O_2 + H_2O \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2SO_4^{2-} + 2H^+}$$

**Stage 2: Oxidation of** $\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}$

$$\mathrm{Fe^{2+} + \frac{1}{4}O_2 + H^+ \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + \frac{1}{2}H_2O}$$

**Stage 3: Accelerated Oxidation by** $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ (Autocatalysis)

$$\mathrm{FeS_2 + 14Fe^{3+} + 8H_2O \rightarrow 15Fe^{2+} + 2SO_4^{2-} + 16H^+}$$

**Stage 4: Precipitation of Goethite**

$$\mathrm{Fe^{3+} + 2H_2O \rightarrow FeOOH\downarrow + 3H^+}$$

::: callout-note
## What is Autocatalysis?

In Stage 3, the generated $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ acts as an oxidant to further dissolve pyrite. This creates a positive feedback loop: $\mathrm{FeS_2 \rightarrow Fe^{2+} \rightarrow Fe^{3+} \rightarrow}$ more $\mathrm{FeS_2}$ oxidized. Once this reaction starts, it accelerates exponentially, which is why AMD is notoriously difficult to stop.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Introducing the REACTION Block

So far, we have used `EQUILIBRIUM_PHASES`, which instructs PHREEQC to "react until equilibrium is reached." Today, we introduce the `REACTION` block. This block is used for **irreversible reactions**, where a specified amount of a substance is forcibly added to the solution, regardless of equilibrium.

```{=html}
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<table style="width:100%; border-collapse:collapse; font-size:0.87rem;">
  <thead>
    <tr style="background:var(--color-background-secondary);">
      <th style="padding:0.6rem 0.8rem; text-align:left; border-bottom:2px solid var(--color-border-primary); color:var(--color-text-primary);">Feature</th>
      <th style="padding:0.6rem 0.8rem; text-align:left; border-bottom:2px solid var(--color-border-primary); color:var(--color-text-primary);">EQUILIBRIUM_PHASES</th>
      <th style="padding:0.6rem 0.8rem; text-align:left; border-bottom:2px solid var(--color-border-primary); color:var(--color-text-primary);">REACTION</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr style="border-bottom:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary);">
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; font-weight:500; color:var(--color-text-primary);">Reaction Type</td>
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; color:var(--color-text-secondary);">Reversible (Equilibrium)</td>
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; color:var(--color-text-secondary);">Irreversible (Forced Addition)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="border-bottom:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); background:var(--color-background-secondary);">
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; font-weight:500; color:var(--color-text-primary);">End Condition</td>
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; color:var(--color-text-secondary);">When SI = 0 is reached</td>
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; color:var(--color-text-secondary);">When specified amount is fully added</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="border-bottom:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary);">
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; font-weight:500; color:var(--color-text-primary);">Common Uses</td>
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; color:var(--color-text-secondary);">Mineral dissolution/precipitation</td>
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; color:var(--color-text-secondary);">Oxidation, combustion, titration</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; font-weight:500; color:var(--color-text-primary);">Application to Pyrite</td>
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; color:#6B7280;">Unsuitable (backward reaction might occur)</td>
      <td style="padding:0.5rem 0.8rem; color:#16A34A; font-weight:500;">✅ Highly Suitable</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
</div>
```

The oxidation of pyrite is thermodynamically irreversible under earth surface conditions—once it dissolves and oxidizes, it does not easily turn back into $\mathrm{FeS_2}$. Therefore, we use `REACTION`.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Role of INCREMENTAL_REACTIONS

The most critical command in this tutorial is **`INCREMENTAL_REACTIONS true`**.

```{=html}
<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:1.2em; margin:1.5em 0;">
  <div style="background:#FEF2F2; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1em; border-top:3px solid #DC2626;">
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:#991B1B; margin-bottom:0.5em;">❌ false (Default)</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.87em; color:#7F1D1D; line-height:1.7;">
      Each step is treated as a <strong>cumulative addition</strong> to the initial solution.<br>
      Intermediate goethite precipitation is ignored, failing to reproduce the correct reaction pathway.
    </div>
  </div>
  <div style="background:#F0FDF4; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1em; border-top:3px solid #16A34A;">
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:#15803D; margin-bottom:0.5em;">✅ true (Used Here)</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.87em; color:#166534; line-height:1.7;">
      Each step is treated as an <strong>increment from the previous step</strong>.<br>
      <code>EQUILIBRIUM_PHASES</code> is evaluated at every step, allowing goethite to precipitate exactly when it becomes supersaturated.
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: callout-important
## Forgetting INCREMENTAL_REACTIONS

If you include `Goethite 0 0` in `EQUILIBRIUM_PHASES` but forget `INCREMENTAL_REACTIONS true`, the SI of goethite might remain supersaturated without precipitating across the steps, yielding completely unrealistic results. **Always use them together.**
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Full PHREEQC Code 

``` phreeqc
# ============================================================
#  DeepFlow #6 - Pyrite Oxidation and AMD Formation
#  Requires INCREMENTAL_REACTIONS true
# ============================================================

SOLUTION 1  "Carbonate Groundwater"
    temp      25
    pH        7
    pe        4
    units     mol/kgw
    Ca        1.5e-2      # 15 mmol/kg (Typical limestone groundwater)
    -water    1

EQUILIBRIUM_PHASES 1
    CO2(g)    -3.5   10   # Equilibrium with atmospheric CO2 (pCO2 = 10^-3.5 atm)
    Goethite   0.0    0   # Goethite precipitation sink (Amount=0)
    Gypsum     0.0    0   # Gypsum precipitation sink (Amount=0)

REACTION 1
    FeS2   4              # Pyrite stoichiometry
    O2    15              # Oxygen

    # Stepwise addition (moles)
    0.0001  0.0003  0.0005  0.001
    0.002   0.005   0.010   moles

INCREMENTAL_REACTIONS  true     # ← Mandatory!

SELECTED_OUTPUT 1
    -file        pyrite_oxidation.sel
    -pH          true
    -pe          true
    -totals      Fe  S  Ca
    -molalities  Fe+2  Fe+3  SO4-2
    -saturation_indices  Goethite  Gypsum  Pyrite
    -equilibrium_phases  Goethite

END
```

::: callout-note
## A Note on Gypsum

We omitted `Gypsum 0 0` from this simulation because typical groundwater $\mathrm{Ca^{2+}}$ concentrations won't reach gypsum supersaturation here. Gypsum precipitation during AMD is generally only observed in highly concentrated environments where both $\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}$ and $\mathrm{Ca^{2+}}$ are extremely abundant.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Results and Interpretation

### Output Data (`pyrite_oxidation.sel`)

Below is the actual output processed with `INCREMENTAL_REACTIONS true`.

```{=html}
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<table style="width:100%; border-collapse:collapse; font-size:0.87rem;">
  <thead>
    <tr style="background:#D97706; color:white;">
      <th style="padding:9px 12px; text-align:center;">Step</th>
      <th style="padding:9px 12px; text-align:center;">pH</th>
      <th style="padding:9px 12px; text-align:center;">pe</th>
      <th style="padding:9px 12px; text-align:center;">Fe (mol/kg)</th>
      <th style="padding:9px 12px; text-align:center;">SO₄ (mol/kg)</th>
      <th style="padding:9px 12px; text-align:center;">SI_Goethite</th>
      <th style="padding:9px 12px; text-align:center;">d_Goethite</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr style="background:#FFF7ED;">
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:600;">1</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">2.865</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">15.54</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">4.2e-9</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">8.0e-4</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">0.000</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-size:0.85em; color:#16A34A;"> 4.0e-4 Precipitating</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#FDFDFD;">
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:600;">2</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">2.291</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">16.35</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">2.3e-7</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">3.2e-3</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">0.000</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-size:0.85em; color:#16A34A;"> 1.2e-3 Precipitating</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#FFF7ED;">
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:600;">3</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">1.973</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">16.81</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">3.3e-6</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">7.2e-3</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">0.000</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-size:0.85em; color:#16A34A;"> 2.0e-3 Precipitating</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#FDFDFD;">
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:600;">4</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">1.695</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">17.21</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">3.7e-5</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">1.5e-2</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">0.000</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-size:0.85em; color:#16A34A;"> 4.0e-3 Precipitating</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#FFF7ED;">
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:600;">5</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">1.440</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">17.59</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">3.5e-4</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">3.1e-2</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">0.000</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-size:0.85em; color:#16A34A;"> 7.6e-3 Precipitating</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#FDFDFD;">
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:600;">6</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">1.173</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">17.98</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">3.7e-3</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">7.1e-2</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">0.000</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-size:0.85em; color:#16A34A;"> 1.6e-2 Precipitating</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#FFF7ED;">
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:600;">7</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; font-weight:600; color:#DC2626;">0.988</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">18.27</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">2.2e-2</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">1.5e-1</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">0.000</td>
      <td style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; font-size:0.85em; color:#DC2626; font-weight:600;"> 2.1e-2 pH &lt; 1! High Hazard</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
</div>
```

### Visualizing the pH Drop

```{=html}
<div style="background:var(--color-background-secondary); border:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); border-radius:8px; padding:1.2rem 1.5rem; margin:1.5rem 0;">
  <div style="font-weight:500; color:var(--color-text-primary); margin-bottom:1rem;">pH vs. Pyrite Addition Step (Simulated Data)</div>
  <svg viewBox="0 0 520 180" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;max-width:520px;display:block;">
    <defs>
      <marker id="arr6" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8" refY="5" markerWidth="5" markerHeight="5" orient="auto-start-reverse">
        <path d="M2 1L8 5L2 9" fill="none" stroke="#9CA3AF" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
      </marker>
    </defs>
    <!-- Axes -->
    <line x1="50" y1="10" x2="50" y2="150" stroke="#9CA3AF" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr6)"/>
    <line x1="50" y1="150" x2="510" y2="150" stroke="#9CA3AF" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr6)"/>
    <!-- Grid + Labels (Y-axis: pH 0 to 3) -->
    <line x1="50" y1="30"  x2="505" y2="30"  stroke="#E5E7EB" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
    <line x1="50" y1="70"  x2="505" y2="70"  stroke="#E5E7EB" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
    <line x1="50" y1="110" x2="505" y2="110" stroke="#E5E7EB" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
    <text x="38" y="34"  font-size="11" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="end">3</text>
    <text x="38" y="74"  font-size="11" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="end">2</text>
    <text x="38" y="114" font-size="11" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="end">1</text>
    <text x="38" y="154" font-size="11" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="end">0</text>
    <text x="20" y="90"  font-size="11" fill="#6B7280" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 20 90)">pH</text>
    <!-- X-axis Labels -->
    <text x="110" y="165" font-size="10" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">1</text>
    <text x="170" y="165" font-size="10" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">2</text>
    <text x="230" y="165" font-size="10" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">3</text>
    <text x="290" y="165" font-size="10" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">4</text>
    <text x="350" y="165" font-size="10" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">5</text>
    <text x="410" y="165" font-size="10" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">6</text>
    <text x="470" y="165" font-size="10" fill="#9CA3AF" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">7</text>
    <text x="280" y="178" font-size="11" fill="#6B7280" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">Pyrite Addition Step →</text>
    <!-- Data Plots -->
    <polyline points="110,35 170,58 230,71 290,82 350,92 410,103 470,110"
              fill="none" stroke="#E11D48" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
    <!-- Data Points -->
    <circle cx="110" cy="35"  r="4" fill="#E11D48"/>
    <circle cx="170" cy="58"  r="4" fill="#E11D48"/>
    <circle cx="230" cy="71"  r="4" fill="#E11D48"/>
    <circle cx="290" cy="82"  r="4" fill="#E11D48"/>
    <circle cx="350" cy="92"  r="4" fill="#E11D48"/>
    <circle cx="410" cy="103" r="4" fill="#E11D48"/>
    <circle cx="470" cy="110" r="4" fill="#E11D48"/>
    <!-- Note on Goethite Precipitation -->
    <line x1="110" y1="15" x2="470" y2="15" stroke="#7C3AED" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
    <circle cx="110" cy="15" r="4" fill="#7C3AED"/>
    <text x="285" y="12" font-size="10" fill="#7C3AED" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">Goethite Precipitating (SI = 0 throughout all steps)</text>
  </svg>
  <div style="font-size:0.82rem; color:var(--color-text-secondary); margin-top:0.5em;">As pyrite is added, the pH drops steeply from Step 1 (pH 2.87) to Step 7 (pH 0.99). Goethite continuously precipitates throughout the entire process.</div>
</div>
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Discussion

### 1. Mechanism of the pH Drop

The pH plummets from 2.87 to 0.99. This is due to the continuous generation of $\mathrm{H^+}$ during pyrite oxidation. The drop accelerates after step 4 (cumulative pyrite \> 1 mmol), which is a direct signature of $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ accumulating and triggering the **autocatalytic oxidation cycle**.

### 2. Precipitation of Goethite

From Step 1 to the final step, **`SI_Goethite` = 0.000** is maintained, and `d_Goethite` (delta/amount transferred) is consistently positive. This means goethite is constantly precipitating.

The cycle is relentless: Pyrite Oxidizes $\rightarrow$ $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ is generated $\rightarrow$ It immediately precipitates as Goethite $\rightarrow$ Releasing even more $\mathrm{H^+}$. This continuous precipitation of goethite is what dyes AMD rivers their characteristic **reddish-brown** (often referred to as "yellow boy").

::: callout-note
## Why is Dissolved Fe So Low?

Notice that dissolved `Fe (mol/kg)` is exceptionally low (e.g., 4.2e-9 mol/kg in step 1). This is because the generated $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ precipitates as goethite almost instantaneously. Even at Step 7, where pH drops below 1, dissolved Fe is only 2.2e-2 mol/kg due to this thermodynamic sink.
:::

### 3. The Rise of 'pe'

The `pe` (negative logarithm of electron activity) increases steadily from 15.54 to 18.27. A higher `pe` indicates a strongly oxidizing environment. Because we are forcibly supplying $\mathrm{O_2}$ in the `REACTION` block, the oxidative power of the solution intensifies as the simulation proceeds.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Simulating AMD Neutralization

A common treatment for AMD is neutralization using crushed limestone (calcite) to raise the pH. We can simulate this using the output from Step 7:

``` phreeqc
# ============================================================
#  AMD Neutralization Simulation
#  Run this as a continuation of the previous code
# ============================================================

SAVE solution 1    # Save the highly acidic final solution
# *IMPORTANT:* Place the SAVE command BEFORE the first END!

USE solution 1

EQUILIBRIUM_PHASES 2
    Calcite   0   10    # Add excess calcite to restore pH

SELECTED_OUTPUT 2
    -file        amd_neutralization.sel
    -pH          true
    -totals      Fe  Ca
    -saturation_indices  Goethite  Calcite

END
```

By adding calcite, the pH is neutralized, forcing any remaining dissolved iron to precipitate as goethite. Check `amd_neutralization.sel` to observe the dramatic pH recovery.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Summary of Key Takeaways

```{=html}
<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(3,1fr); gap:1em; margin:1.5em 0;">
  <div style="background:var(--color-background-secondary); border:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); border-radius:10px; padding:1.1em; text-align:center; border-bottom:3px solid #D97706;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-weight:600; color:#92400E; margin-bottom:0.4em;">REACTION</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.82em; color:var(--color-text-secondary); line-height:1.5;">Defines irreversible reactions to forcibly add specific moles of reactants.</div>
  </div>
  <div style="background:var(--color-background-secondary); border:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); border-radius:10px; padding:1.1em; text-align:center; border-bottom:3px solid #16A34A;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-weight:600; color:#15803D; margin-bottom:0.4em;">INCREMENTAL_REACTIONS</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.82em; color:var(--color-text-secondary); line-height:1.5;">Evaluates equilibrium at each step. Essential for accurately modeling reaction pathways.</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: callout-tip
## Next Time: #7 Solubility Diagrams (Gibbsite)

Next, we will explore the `Fix_H+` technique within the `PHASES` block to artificially lock the pH at various values. We will calculate the solubility of aluminum across pH 3 to 14, visualizing the amphoteric nature of Gibbsite in a comprehensive solubility diagram.
:::

*Other articles in this series:*

- [#1 Installation and Initial Calculation](../phreeqc-part1/index-en.html)
- [#2 Analyzing Seawater with Speciation](../phreeqc-part2/index-en.html)
- [#3 Mineral Equilibrium and Temperature Effects](../phreeqc-part3/index-en.html)
- [#4 Calcite–CO₂ Interaction (Open vs. Closed Systems)](../phreeqc-part4/index-en.html)
- [#5 Mixing Groundwater and Seawater](../phreeqc-part5/index-en.html)
- **#6 Pyrite Oxidation and AMD Formation** (This article)
- [#7 Solubility Diagrams (Gibbsite)](../phreeqc-part7/index-en.html)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*DeepFlow \| Science beneath the surface*
